cases.title: Masked Shock on Beta-Blockers
cases.presentation: An 80-year-old fell down stairs and has a tender abdomen and pelvic pain. He looks pale and diaphoretic, but his heart rate is only 78 and his blood pressure is 118/76. His medication list includes metoprolol.
cases.question: How should you interpret these vitals?
options: He is stable—the heart rate and pressure are normal
His beta-blocker is masking compensatory tachycardia; pallor and diaphoresis suggest shock—treat aggressively and transport
cases.explanation: Metoprolol blunts the tachycardic response, so a 'normal' heart rate is misleading. Pallor and diaphoresis with a concerning mechanism indicate shock from likely intra-abdominal/pelvic hemorrhage. Treat for shock, anticipate rapid decompensation, and transport to a trauma center.
cases.clinicalPearl: Don't trust the heart rate in a patient on beta-blockers or with a pacemaker—look at skin, mentation, and mechanism.

cases.title: Minor Bump, Major Risk
cases.presentation: A 76-year-old on apixaban tripped and struck her head on a doorframe. She has a small forehead hematoma, is alert, and has a normal neurologic exam. She wants to refuse transport.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate guidance?
options: Agree she is fine and clear her on scene
Explain the high risk of delayed intracranial bleeding on anticoagulants, strongly recommend transport, and monitor closely
Tell her head injuries never matter at her age
Give a fluid bolus and leave
cases.explanation: Anticoagulants greatly increase the risk of intracranial hemorrhage, which can be delayed even after a minor mechanism with a currently normal exam. Strongly recommend transport for evaluation/observation and document the discussion if she still declines.
cases.clinicalPearl: A normal exam does not exclude bleeding in an anticoagulated elder—delayed intracranial hemorrhage is the danger.

cases.title: Immobilizing the Kyphotic Spine
cases.presentation: An 84-year-old with severe kyphosis needs spinal motion restriction after a fall. When laid flat on a rigid board, her head hangs well off the surface and she is in obvious distress.
cases.question: What is the correct technique?
options: Strap her head down forcibly to meet the board
Pad the voids behind the head and back to support her natural curvature in a comfortable neutral position
Apply heavy traction to straighten the spine
cases.explanation: Forcing a kyphotic spine flat can cause injury and severe pain. Pad the gaps behind the head and back to maintain her baseline curvature in a comfortable, neutral position, and consider a conforming device for transport.
cases.clinicalPearl: Immobilize the elderly spine in its natural position—pad the curves, never force them flat.
